name: Receive Difficult News on Behalf of the Operator
description: Take a hard outcome — a test result, a claim denial, a rejection, a refusal — on the operator's behalf with composure, get every piece of information they will need, and stay regulated enough to ask the next-step questions before the line closes.
disclaimer: If the news is a medical or mental-health crisis (a positive result for a serious illness, a mental-health danger signal), follow the operator's documented preferences. If none exist, deliver the news in person/by warm channel rather than text, and ensure the operator is not alone when they get it.

context.when_to_use: You are the intermediary on a call where the rep is delivering news the operator will find hard — a denied claim, a no, a difficult diagnosis being relayed by a clinic, a 'we've decided to go another direction.' The operator is not on the line.
preconditions: Operator has authorised you to receive sensitive information on their behalf.
You have a way to deliver the news back to the operator (channel + recommended timing).
You know which DETAILS the operator will need beyond the headline (dates, reasons, appeal windows, next steps).

principles: Your job is to be a clean conduit. Emotion belongs to the operator, not to you on their behalf.
The rep is often relieved that you are composed. Composed callers get more information than tearful ones, fairly or not.
The first 30 seconds after the news are the information-richest. Get the questions out before the rep wraps.
Repeat the news back, verbatim, before reacting. This locks in accuracy and buys you composure-time.
Do not editorialise. 'That's awful' or 'wow that's unfair' belongs in your debrief to the operator, not the call.
Plan the operator delivery while still on the call. The rep gave you data; now you need delivery channel + framing.

phrases.acknowledge_and_lock_in: Thank you for telling me. Let me read it back to make sure I have it right: [exact words]. Did I get that correctly?
phrases.ask_for_reason: Could you walk me through the reason — I want to be able to explain it accurately to [operator].
phrases.ask_for_next_steps: What are the options from here? Is there an appeal, a re-test, or a different path we can consider?
phrases.ask_for_timing: What's the timeline — when does this take effect, and what's the deadline if there's a way to respond?
phrases.ask_for_written: Will this come in writing — letter, email, portal message? When should we expect it?
phrases.request_contact_for_followup: If [operator] has questions after I deliver this, who is the best person and number for them to call?

tactics.name: Lock the headline before reacting
tactics.when: Immediately after the rep delivers the news.
tactics.script: Use `acknowledge_and_lock_in`. Read it back literally. This protects accuracy AND gives you a beat to regulate.

tactics.name: Sweep for the four questions
tactics.script: Reason / next steps / timing / written confirmation. Get all four before the call closes — they're harder to get on a callback. Use the four `ask_for_*` phrases.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — REP IS UNCOMFORTABLE
tactics.when: Rep speaks quickly, uses softeners ('unfortunately', 'I'm so sorry to have to tell you'), or volunteers extra context.
tactics.script: Slow down. Thank them once, sincerely. Reps who feel bad sometimes share information they're not technically supposed to — make space for it without prompting.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — REP IS DETACHED
tactics.when: Rep reads it like an item on a script, no softener.
tactics.script: Do not perform emotion to pull warmth out of them. Stay calm and businesslike — you'll get more information by matching their register than by guilting them.

tactics.name: Capture the human details too
tactics.when: Towards the end of the call.
tactics.script: Get the rep's name, employee ID, the time, and any case/reference number. Get the spelling. This is what makes a credible report to the operator and a credible appeal.

tactics.name: Plan the operator delivery before hanging up
tactics.script: Decide: which channel (call vs. text vs. in-person), when (now vs. let them finish work), and what to lead with. Do not deliver until you've decided. A botched delivery is sometimes worse than a delayed one.

boundaries: Do NOT relay the news to the operator inside this call by three-way without their explicit standing consent.
Do NOT speculate to the rep ('they're going to be devastated', 'this is going to ruin them'). Keep the operator's interior private.
Do NOT argue with the rep on the spot. Take the information cleanly; argue in writing on appeal.
Do NOT delay delivering the news to the operator unreasonably — they may need the time window to act.
Do NOT improve the news for the operator's sake. They need it accurate; reframing belongs after the facts.
success_signals: You can recite the news back verbatim.
You have all four facts (reason / next steps / timing / written).
You have a case number and rep ID.
The rep ends the call somewhere between 'I'm sorry' and 'have a good day' — neither was performance.
failure_signals: You realise after hanging up you missed the timeline.
You editorialised mid-call and the rep got formal again.
You started crying or audibly reacting — composure is the job here.
The news isn't in writing and you have no way to confirm it.

exit_strategy.on_success: End politely, thank the rep for being thorough. Hang up. PAUSE for 30 seconds before contacting the operator — gather your phrasing.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If you missed information, call back and ask for the same rep if possible: 'I want to make sure I represent this correctly to [operator] — can you confirm [thing I missed]?'
follow_ups: Deliver to the operator via the channel + timing you decided, with the headline first and the four facts ready.
Save a written record (your notes + any letter that arrives) — operator may need it for appeal, insurance, HR.
Schedule a check-in with the operator 24-48h later — first-day reactions and second-day reactions differ.

required_user_info: Standing preference: how does the operator want hard news delivered (call vs text, immediately vs end of day)?
Who else, if anyone, is authorised to know?
Any appeal / response timeline the operator wants to defend by default.
